exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'me' (1st person singular)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'me' is με. Example: Με βλέπεις. (You see me.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'you' (2nd person singular)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'you' (singular) is σε. Example: Σε βλέπω. (I see you.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'him' (3rd person singular masculine)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'him' is τον. Example: Τον ξέρω. (I know him.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'her' (3rd person singular feminine)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'her' is την. Example: Την βλέπω. (I see her.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'it' (3rd person singular neuter)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'it' (neuter) is το. Example: Το βλέπω. (I see it.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'us' (1st person plural)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'us' is μας. Example: Μας βλέπει. (She sees us.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'you all' (2nd person plural)
exercises.explanation: The direct object (accusative) pronoun for 'you all' is σας. Example: Σας βλέπω. (I see you all.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'them' (3rd person plural masculine/mixed)
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'them' (masculine or mixed group) is τους. Example: Τους βλέπω. (I see them.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'them' (3rd person plural feminine)
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'them' (feminine plural) is τις. Example: Τις βλέπω. (I see them - referring to a group of women/feminine nouns.)

exercises.header: Direct object pronoun for 'them' (3rd person plural neuter)
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'them' (neuter plural) is τα. Example: Τα βλέπω. (I see them - referring to neuter nouns like παιδιά, βιβλία.)

exercises.header: ___ βλέπεις. (You see me.)
exercises.explanation: Με is the direct object pronoun for 'me'. It goes before the verb in statements: Με βλέπεις.

exercises.header: ___ ξέρω. (I know him.)
exercises.explanation: Τον is the direct object pronoun for 'him'. It goes before the verb: Τον ξέρω.

exercises.header: Αυτός ___ καλεί. (He calls her.)
exercises.explanation: Την is the direct object pronoun for 'her'. Αυτός την καλεί. (He calls her.)

exercises.header: Δεν ___ βλέπω. (I don't see you, singular.)
exercises.explanation: Σε is the direct object pronoun for 'you' (singular). Δεν σε βλέπω.

exercises.header: Η μητέρα ___ αγαπά. (The mother loves us.)
exercises.explanation: Μας is the direct object pronoun for 'us'. Η μητέρα μας αγαπά.

exercises.header: Θα ___ τηλεφωνήσω. (I will call you, plural.)
exercises.explanation: Σας is the direct object pronoun for 'you' (plural/formal). Θα σας τηλεφωνήσω.

exercises.header: Δεν ___ ακούω. (I don't hear them — referring to neuter objects.)
exercises.explanation: Τα is the direct object pronoun for neuter plural 'them'. Δεν τα ακούω.

exercises.header: Η Μαρία ___ περιμένει. (Maria waits for them — masculine group.)
exercises.explanation: Τους is the direct object pronoun for masculine/mixed plural 'them'. Η Μαρία τους περιμένει.

exercises.header: 'You see me' — which pronoun represents 'me'?
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'me' is με. Με βλέπεις. (You see me.)

exercises.header: 'They hear us' — which pronoun represents 'us'?
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'us' is μας. Μας ακούνε. (They hear us.)

exercises.header: 'She sees you (singular)' — which pronoun represents 'you'?
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'you' (singular) is σε. Αυτή σε βλέπει.

exercises.header: 'He loves them (feminine plural)' — which pronoun represents 'them'?
exercises.explanation: The direct object pronoun for 'them' (feminine plural) is τις. Αυτός τις αγαπά.

exercises.header: Which pronoun replaces 'τον Γιώργη' as a direct object?
exercises.explanation: A masculine singular noun like Γιώργης is replaced by τον as a direct object. Τον ξέρω. (I know him/George.)

exercises.header: Which pronoun replaces 'την Ελένη' as a direct object?
exercises.explanation: A feminine singular noun like Ελένη is replaced by την as a direct object. Την ξέρω. (I know her/Eleni.)

exercises.header: In a normal statement, where does the personal object pronoun go?

options.c: At the end of the sentence

exercises.explanation: Personal object pronouns go BEFORE the verb in normal statements. Example: Τον βλέπω. (I see him.) — only imperatives and gerunds place them after.
